worth_it_explanation: Ad scheduling decides when an account spends, and a mismatch wastes budget on hours the business cannot serve or convert. The checks are quick to run. The ad schedule view and the hour-of-day and day-of-week segments sit a few clicks apart in the same account, and they show whether a schedule exists, whether bid adjustments line up with when conversions happen, and whether the account time zone matches the business. The fixes are configuration changes, not rebuilds, and they take effect on the next serving window.
intro: Ad scheduling controls the hours and days an account's ads run, and the bid adjustments that ride on that schedule control how aggressively the account bids at each time. A campaign with no schedule runs every hour at full budget, including hours when a phone line is closed or a store cannot fulfill. A schedule with no bid adjustments treats a high-converting Tuesday morning the same as a low-converting Saturday night. The account time zone sits underneath all of it because every scheduled hour is read against that time zone rather than the customer's. These checks confirm that the schedule reflects when the business actually converts, that bid adjustments follow the conversion pattern, and that the account time zone matches the business it runs for.

audit_checks.title: Campaigns run every hour for a business that only serves or converts during set hours
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Budget spent during hours the business cannot answer, fulfill, or close pulls spend away from the hours it can. For a call-driven or appointment-driven advertiser, clicks that land on a closed line rarely convert, so the cost per acquisition rises while the schedule sits at its default of all hours.

educate.base:
Ad scheduling lets a campaign show ads only on the days and hours you choose, rather than the default of running every hour of every day. A business whose conversions depend on a staffed phone line, a booking desk, or same-day fulfillment converts on a schedule, and when the ad schedule ignores that pattern, the account keeps paying for clicks during hours the business cannot act on them.

The cost is indirect but real. A click at 2 a.m. on a closed line still draws from the budget, and the lead it might have produced goes to voicemail or a contact form that sits unanswered until morning. Spend that lands in dead hours is spend that did not run during the hours the business converts. On a capped daily budget, the two compete directly.

Two patterns separate a deliberate all-hours schedule from an oversight. The first is whether conversions cluster into a window. If the hour-of-day and day-of-week segments show conversions concentrated in business hours with little after-hours activity, an all-hours schedule is spending into hours that do not pay back. The second is whether the business can even act on an after-hours lead. A self-serve store that completes a purchase at any hour is different from a clinic that books appointments only when the desk is staffed.

A few signs suggest a schedule is warranted:
  • Conversions in the hour-of-day segment fall off sharply outside business hours
  • The primary conversion is a phone call or a form that a person follows up on, not a self-serve checkout
  • The campaign shows the default all-hours schedule with no day or hour restrictions
You may want to consider adding an ad schedule that matches the hours the business converts, or applying bid adjustments rather than hard cutoffs if some after-hours value still exists. A hard cutoff removes the hours entirely, while a downward bid adjustment keeps them at a lower bid.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: A self-serve store can take an order at any hour, so a hard schedule cutoff risks turning off hours that still convert. You may want to read the hour-of-day conversion segment before restricting anything. If overnight orders still come in at an acceptable cost, a downward bid adjustment fits better than a cutoff since it keeps the hours live at a reduced bid rather than removing them.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: A lead-generation account often depends on a person following up, so a click at an hour when no one answers converts worse and can sour the lead by the time it is contacted. You may want to align the schedule with the hours the team responds, or at least apply a downward bid adjustment to the unstaffed hours so the account bids less aggressively when a fast follow-up is not possible.

investigate.intro: Read the campaign's ad schedule and compare it against the hour-of-day and day-of-week conversion segments to see whether spend is running in hours that do not convert.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open a campaign, then open 'Ad schedule' from the campaign's section menu, and note whether a schedule is set or whether it shows the default of all hours.
Open the campaign's reporting and segment performance by 'Hour of day' and by 'Day of week'. Note which hours and days carry conversions and which carry spend without conversions.

Compare the two. If spend lands in hours that show few or no conversions, and the conversion is one the business can only act on during set hours, the all-hours schedule is a candidate for restriction or a downward bid adjustment.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per campaign, whether an ad schedule is set, whether the primary conversion depends on staffed hours, and whether the hour-of-day segment shows spend in low-converting hours. A campaign whose conversions span all hours, or whose conversion is self-serve, passes even with an all-hours schedule. A campaign with conversions concentrated in business hours, a staff-dependent conversion, and meaningful spend in dead hours is a fail recorded with the campaign name.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the ad schedule status, the conversion type, and the hour-of-day spend pattern for each campaign above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that several of {{site_name}}'s campaigns run on the default all-hours schedule while their conversions cluster into business hours. Spend is landing in hours when the business cannot act on a lead, which pulls budget away from the hours that convert and raises the effective cost per acquisition. {{voice_recommend}} adding an ad schedule that matches the hours {{site_name}} converts, or applying downward bid adjustments to the low-converting hours where some after-hours value remains. After the change, segment the campaigns by hour of day again and confirm spend has shifted toward the converting hours.

audit_checks.title: An ad schedule exists but carries no bid adjustments where conversion rate varies by time
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When every scheduled hour bids the same despite a clear conversion-rate spread across the day, the account underbids its best hours and overbids its weak ones. The result is missed volume in the windows that convert and wasted spend in the windows that do not, both correctable through adjustments the schedule already supports.

educate.base:
An ad schedule can do more than turn hours on and off. On campaigns that use the Maximize clicks strategy, it can carry bid adjustments that raise or lower the bid for specific days and times, so a high-converting window bids up and a weak window bids down. A schedule that restricts hours but applies no adjustments treats every scheduled hour as equal, even when the conversion rate across those hours is not.

The reason this goes unnoticed is that the schedule looks complete. Hours are set, ads run, and nothing flags the missing adjustments. Underneath, the account bids the same amount at 9 a.m. on a converting Tuesday as it does at 9 p.m. on a quiet Sunday, so it competes too little for the clicks worth winning and too hard for the ones worth less.

Bid adjustments apply on manual and Maximize-clicks bidding. Accounts on a conversion-based Smart Bidding strategy, such as Target cost per acquisition (CPA) or Target return on ad spend (ROAS), already factor time of day into the auction-time bid, so a manual ad schedule adjustment on those strategies is ignored. The check is most relevant on campaigns where the bid is set manually or by Maximize clicks because there the human-set adjustment is the only thing tilting the bid by time.

A few signs suggest adjustments are worth setting:
  • The hour-of-day or day-of-week segment shows a clear spread in conversion rate across the schedule
  • The campaign uses manual bidding or Maximize clicks rather than a conversion-based Smart Bidding strategy
  • The ad schedule shows restricted hours but every entry sits at the default zero percent adjustment
You may want to consider setting bid adjustments that follow the conversion-rate pattern, biasing the bid up in the strong windows and down in the weak ones. On a Smart Bidding strategy, the more durable move is to confirm the strategy has enough conversion data to read the time pattern itself rather than adding adjustments it will ignore.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: A store's conversion rate often climbs in evening browsing hours and dips during the workday, so flat bids across an all-day schedule leave the strong evening window underbid. You may want to read the hour-of-day conversion-rate segment and set upward adjustments on the converting hours, while keeping the math in view, where adjustments multiply against location and device adjustments already in place.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: A lead-generation account frequently converts best during business hours when prospects are at a desk, and a flat-bid schedule misses the chance to bid up that window. You may want to set an upward adjustment on the hours your conversion rate peaks, and a downward one on the off-hours, so the account competes hardest when a submitted lead is most likely to close.

learn_more.source_title: Set up an ad schedule bid adjustment

investigate.intro: Confirm the campaign's bidding strategy, then compare its ad schedule bid adjustments against the conversion-rate spread in the hour-of-day and day-of-week segments.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open the campaign and note its bidding strategy. Bid adjustments on an ad schedule apply on manual bidding and Maximize clicks, and are ignored on conversion-based Smart Bidding strategies such as Target CPA or Target ROAS.
Open 'Ad schedule' for the campaign and read the 'Bid adj.' column. Note whether each entry sits at the default zero percent or carries a set adjustment.

Segment the campaign by 'Hour of day' and 'Day of week' and read the conversion rate across the schedule. If the conversion rate spreads clearly across hours while the bid adjustments all sit at zero, the schedule is not tilting the bid toward the hours that convert.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per campaign with an ad schedule, the bidding strategy, whether bid adjustments are set, and whether the hour-of-day conversion rate varies materially. A campaign on a conversion-based Smart Bidding strategy passes because auction-time bidding handles the time pattern. A manual or Maximize-clicks campaign with a clear conversion-rate spread and all adjustments at zero is a fail recorded with the campaign name.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the bidding strategy, the bid adjustment values, and the hour-of-day conversion-rate spread for each scheduled campaign above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} has ad schedules in place on manual or Maximize-clicks campaigns but the scheduled hours all carry the default zero percent bid adjustment, while the hour-of-day data shows conversion rate varying across those hours. The account bids the same in its strong and weak windows, so it underbids the hours that convert and overbids the hours that do not. {{voice_recommend}} setting bid adjustments that follow the conversion-rate pattern on these campaigns, and confirming any campaign on conversion-based Smart Bidding has enough data for auction-time bidding to read the pattern itself. After the change, recheck the schedule's bid adjustments against the hour-of-day conversion rate.

audit_checks.title: The account time zone does not match the business, shifting every scheduled hour
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Every scheduled hour and every hour-of-day report is read against the account time zone, so a mismatch shifts the whole schedule by the offset. A daypart meant for a local morning fires at the wrong clock time, and the conversion data the schedule is tuned from is labeled with hours that are off by the same amount.

educate.base:
Every Google Ads account has a single time zone, set when the account is created. Ad schedules, hour-of-day reporting, and dayparting are all read against that time zone, not the customer's local time and not the time zone of whoever manages the account. When the account time zone does not match the market the account serves, the entire schedule shifts by the offset between them.

The effect compounds because the data and the schedule move together. A daypart built to bid up a local 9 a.m. peak fires at 9 a.m. in the account time zone, which may be 6 a.m. or noon for the customer. The hour-of-day report that the practitioner reads to find that peak is also stamped in the account time zone, so the schedule can look internally consistent while sitting offset from the real-world hours it was meant to target. An account managed from one region for a business in another is the usual setup where this hides.

The constraint that makes this matter is that the time zone cannot be changed on a standard serving account. Google does not support time zone changes on serving accounts, so an account created in the wrong zone stays there unless it is rebuilt in a new account with the correct zone, which does not carry the history forward. That permanence raises the stakes of catching the mismatch and of building schedules with the offset in mind when the zone cannot be corrected.

A few signs point to a time zone worth checking:
  • The account serves a market in a different time zone from the one shown in the account settings
  • Ad schedule dayparts appear shifted from the business's local hours when spot-checked
  • The account was created by a team in a different region from the business it advertises
You may want to confirm the account time zone against the business's market early because the setting is fixed once the account is serving. Where the zone is already wrong and cannot be changed, you may want to build ad schedules and read hour-of-day data with the offset applied, so the dayparts land on the customer's real hours.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: A store shipping nationally spans several time zones, so a single account time zone is always an approximation rather than a mismatch to fix. The risk is sharper for a store concentrated in one region whose account sits in another zone, where the evening shopping peak is read and scheduled against the wrong clock. You may want to confirm the account zone against the bulk of the customer base before tuning any daypart.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: A lead-generation business serving one local market is the case where a time zone mismatch bites hardest because the converting hours are tied to when local prospects are active and when the team answers. You may want to confirm the account time zone matches that local market, and where the account is already fixed to the wrong zone, build the schedule with the offset so the dayparts track the local business hours.

learn_more.source_title: About your Google Ads time zone setting

investigate.intro: Confirm the account time zone in settings, then check whether it matches the market the account serves and whether the ad schedules are built against it.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open the account settings and note the account time zone. This is a single account-wide value set at creation.

Compare it against the market the account serves, for example the location targeting and the bulk of the customer base. Note any offset between the account time zone and the business's local time zone.

Open a campaign's ad schedule and spot-check a daypart against the business's stated hours. If the account is offset from the market, confirm whether the schedule was built in account time or accidentally in the business's local time. Reading the hour-of-day report against the wrong zone leads to dayparts that land at the wrong clock time.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the account time zone, the market's time zone, and whether the ad schedules account for any offset. An account whose time zone matches its market passes. An account offset from its market whose schedules are built without accounting for the offset is a fail, noted with the offset and whether the zone can be corrected. A serving account's time zone is fixed and may require a rebuild rather than a settings change.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the account time zone, the market time zone, and whether the schedules account for the offset to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s account time zone does not match the market it serves, which shifts every ad schedule and every hour-of-day report by the offset between them. Dayparts meant for the customer's local hours fire at the wrong clock time, and the conversion data the schedule is tuned from is stamped in the wrong zone. {{voice_recommend}} confirming the account time zone against the market, and where it is correct at the account level, rebuilding the schedules so the dayparts track local hours. The account time zone cannot be changed on a serving account, so where it is already wrong, {{voice_recommend}} applying the offset within the schedule rather than expecting a settings change to fix it.
